questions.question: Define a distributed system and list its key characteristics.
keyPoints: Collection of independent computers appearing as single system
questions.modelAnswer: A distributed system is a collection of independent computers that appears to its users as a single coherent system. Key characteristics include: (1) Multiple autonomous nodes with local memory, (2) Communication via message passing over network, (3) No shared memory, (4) Common goal coordination, (5) Transparency hiding distribution complexity.

questions.question: Explain the different types of transparency in distributed systems with examples.
keyPoints: Access transparency - hides data representation differences
Failure transparency - hides failures and recovery
questions.modelAnswer: Distributed systems provide multiple types of transparency: (1) Access Transparency hides differences in data representation (e.g., big vs little endian), (2) Location Transparency hides where resources are physically located (e.g., DNS hiding IP addresses), (3) Migration Transparency hides that resources may move during operation, (4) Replication Transparency hides that data is replicated across nodes, (5) Concurrency Transparency hides that resources may be shared by multiple users, (6) Failure Transparency hides failures and recovery of components.

questions.question: Compare and contrast centralized systems with distributed systems.
questions.modelAnswer: Centralized systems have a single server handling all requests, while distributed systems use multiple nodes. Key differences: (1) Failure: Centralized has single point of failure; distributed continues despite node failures. (2) Scalability: Centralized limited by single machine; distributed scales horizontally. (3) Complexity: Centralized simpler to design; distributed requires handling network failures, concurrency. (4) Latency: Centralized offers consistent low latency; distributed has variable network latency. (5) Cost: Centralized requires expensive powerful hardware; distributed uses commodity servers.

questions.question: What are the major challenges in designing distributed systems? Explain each with examples.
keyPoints: No global clock - clock drift, event ordering
questions.modelAnswer: Major challenges include: (1) Network Unreliability - messages can be lost, duplicated, or delayed; network partitions can isolate nodes. (2) Partial Failures - individual nodes can fail independently, making it hard to determine if a node is slow or dead. (3) Concurrency - multiple operations happen simultaneously, leading to race conditions and requiring synchronization. (4) No Global Clock - each node has its own clock that can drift, making event ordering difficult. (5) Heterogeneity - nodes may have different hardware, OS, and software, requiring interoperability solutions.

questions.question: List and explain the motivations for using distributed systems.
keyPoints: Scalability - add nodes for more capacity
questions.modelAnswer: Key motivations for distributed systems: (1) Scalability - handle growing workload by adding more nodes horizontally. (2) Reliability - system continues operating despite individual component failures through redundancy. (3) Performance - parallel processing across multiple nodes improves throughput. (4) Resource Sharing - share hardware, software, and data across geographic locations. (5) Cost Efficiency - use multiple commodity servers instead of expensive supercomputers.

questions.question: Explain Lamport's definition of distributed systems and discuss its implications.
keyPoints: Quote: failure of unknown computer affects yours
questions.modelAnswer: Leslie Lamport defined a distributed system as 'one in which the failure of a computer you didn't even know existed can render your own computer unusable.' This highlights: (1) Complex interdependencies between components, (2) Failures can cascade across the system, (3) Users may be unaware of the system's true complexity, (4) Need for robust fault tolerance mechanisms, (5) Importance of understanding the full system architecture.
